answer:
Iqbal's *Saqaliya* (Bang-e-Dara Part 2, ch. 21) and the Old English *The Ruin* (Old English Elegies, ch. 16) are both poignant city-elegies contemplating decayed urban grandeur, but they construct their "architectures of loss"—the poetic frameworks for evoking vanished civilizations—along divergent lines: one through vivid historical and emotional invocation, the other through stark, material inventory.[^1][^2]

### Shared Foundations: Invocation, Contrast, and Endurance
Both poems open by arresting the gaze on ruins, framing loss as a spectacle demanding response. *Saqaliya* begins with a direct apostrophe summoning tears: "Weep now, unburden your heart, O eye that rains down tears of blood, / For there, in sight, lies the grave of Hejazi civilization."[^3] Similarly, *The Ruin* marvels at physical remnants: "Wondrous is this wall-stone, by Wyrd broken; / Strongholds shattered, the work of giants crumbles."[^4] This shared entry-point establishes architecture as a mnemonic trigger: walls and graves index prior vitality.

Each then pivots to *ubi sunt* contrast (where are the mighty now?), rebuilding the lost city imaginatively before demolishing it anew. Iqbal resurrects Hejazi (Arab-Islamic) Sicily's imperial prowess—"Here once was the tumult of those desert-dwellers, / For whose ships the sea itself was but a playground"—only to silence its "thunderous echoes" like the "great Takbir's call."[^5] *The Ruin* catalogues Roman engineering marvels—"Bright were the city-halls, the bath-houses many"—now overtaken by "mighty Wyrd," plague, and "ruin [that] has crushed the ground."[^6] Loss is temporal: Iqbal's through cultural eclipse (echoing laments for Baghdad and Granada); the Old English poet's through entropic fate across "a hundred generations."[^7]

Endurance motifs bind them. Ruins persist as eloquent witnesses: Sicily's "silence... holds its own eloquence"; the wall "endured... reign after reign, / standing against storms."[^8] Both imply future transmission—Iqbal vows to "carry you as a gift to Hindustan" to "make others weep"; *The Ruin* leaves the observer pondering builders' "ingenious skill."[^9]

### Divergent Structures: Emotional Edifice vs. Material Scaffold
Iqbal erects an *emotional architecture*, layering verse stanzas as a reliquary for Muslim Sicily's spiritual and martial glory. The poem's 17 paragraphs ascend from personal grief to panoramic history (ships, emperors, "world renewed"), personify the island ("Ah, O Sicily! You are the honor of the sea"), invoke poetic forebears (Shiraz's nightingale, Dagh, Ibn Badrun), and descend to the poet's inheritance ("To sorrow-fated Iqbal, your mourning was bequeathed").[^10] Loss is dynamic and relational: a "dead world came to life" via Islamic renewal, now entombed, demanding communal mourning carried eastward. Architecture symbolizes civilizational *khudi* (selfhood)—vibrant, world-shaking—entombed by history's forgetfulness.

*The Ruin*'s *material architecture*, in contrast, scaffolds loss prosaically across three sprawling paragraphs, dissecting the site like an archaeological survey: walls ("sheared and sunken"), roofs ("toppled"), gates ("plundered"), mortar ("frost on"), baths ("hot streams").[^11] No named agents or direct address; *wyrd* (fate) is the impersonal architect of decay, grinding "ancient work" into "mounds." Joy is corporeal—"many a mead-hall full of human joys," "warrior... proud and wine-flushed"—yielding to universal mortality ("armies fell to earth"). The baths' lingering heat evokes futile luxury, underscoring *anic* (impermanence) without redemption. This yields a stoic, observational frame: ruins as *entra geweorc* (giants' works), evoking awe at nature's triumph over hubris.

### Arches of Resonance
Iqbal's loss is recoverable through memory and verse, a call to revive Hejazi fire; *The Ruin*'s is inexorable, fate's masonry entombing all. Yet both poems' "architectures" transform rubble into revelation: Sicily's grave weeps blood for Islamic renaissance; the wall-stone whispers of wyrd's dominion.[^12]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 (*Saqaliya*), [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16 (*The Ruin*), [/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/).

[^3]: *Saqaliya*, [p-08760e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e).

[^4]: *The Ruin*, [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^5]: *Saqaliya*, [p-13e58a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-13e58a), [p-26728b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-26728b).

[^6]: *The Ruin*, [p-ba51da](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-ba51da).

[^7]: *Saqaliya*, [p-02c3ef](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-02c3ef); *The Ruin*, [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^8]: *Saqaliya*, [p-3ff1f9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-3ff1f9); *The Ruin*, [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^9]: *Saqaliya*, [p-665fb2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-665fb2); *The Ruin*, [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd).

[^10]: *Saqaliya*, full chapter [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/).

[^11]: *The Ruin*, [p-d0d2bd](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-d0d2bd) to [p-dfdbda](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-dfdbda).

[^12]: *Saqaliya*, [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/); *The Ruin*, [/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/).
